slides.title: Introduction to Ruby Programming
slides.subtitle:
From Beginner to Ruby Developer
Elegant, Productive, and Developer-Friendly

box.title: Dynamic, open-source programming language focused on simplicity and productivity

note.text: MINASWAN: Matz is nice and so we are nice - Ruby community philosophy

list.text: Readable Syntax - Easy to write and understand

box.code:
# Install rbenv and ruby-build on macOS
brew install rbenv ruby-build

# Add to shell configuration
echo 'eval "$(rbenv init -)"' >> ~/.zshrc

# Install latest Ruby
rbenv install 3.2.2
rbenv global 3.2.2

# Verify installation
ruby -v
# => ruby 3.2.2 (2023-03-30 revision e51014f9c0) [x86_64-darwin22]

# Install bundler
gem install bundler
box.title: Recommended installation method

box.code:
# Variables (dynamic typing)
name = "Alice"
age = 30
price = 19.99
is_admin = true

# Strings
first_name = "John"
last_name = 'Doe'
full_name = "#{first_name} #{last_name}"

# Numbers
integer = 42
float = 3.14
big_number = 1_000_000

# Booleans
true_value = true
false_value = false
nil_value = nil

# Output
puts "Hello, #{name}!"
print "This doesn't add newline"
p variable # Debug output with inspection
box.title: Basic Ruby syntax

box.code:
# Single line comment

=begin
Multi-line comment
This is less common in practice
=end

# Naming conventions
variable_name = "snake_case"     # variables and methods
CONSTANT_NAME = "uppercase"      # constants
ClassName = "CamelCase"          # classes and modules

# Method names should be descriptive
def calculate_total_price
  # method body
end

# Use ? for predicate methods (return boolean)
def admin?
  @role == 'admin'
end

# Use ! for dangerous methods (modify receiver)
def save!
  save || raise "Failed to save!"
end
box.title: Ruby coding conventions

box.code:
# if/elsif/else
age = 20
if age >= 18
  puts "Adult"
elsif age >= 13
  puts "Teenager"
else
  puts "Child"
end

# unless (opposite of if)
unless user.logged_in?
  redirect_to login_path
end

# case/when (switch statement)
grade = 'B'
case grade
when 'A'
  puts "Excellent!"
when 'B'
  puts "Good job!"
when 'C'
  puts "You passed"
else
  puts "See me after class"
end

# Ternary operator
result = score > 50 ? "Pass" : "Fail"

# Modifier form (if/unless at end)
puts "Hello" if user.active?
box.title: Ruby conditionals

box.code:
# Times loop (execute specific number of times)
5.times { |i| puts "Iteration #{i}" }

# Each loop (most common)
[1, 2, 3].each { |num| puts num * 2 }

# While loop
counter = 0
while counter < 5
  puts counter
  counter += 1
end

# Until loop
counter = 0
until counter >= 5
  puts counter
  counter += 1
end

# For loop (less common in Ruby)
for i in 1..5
  puts i
end

# Loop control
break    # exit loop
next     # skip to next iteration
redo     # restart current iteration
retry    # restart entire loop (in rescue)
box.title: Looping in Ruby

box.code:
# Basic method definition
def greet(name)
  "Hello, #{name}!"
end

puts greet("Alice")  # => "Hello, Alice!"

# Method with default parameters
def greet(name = "World")
  "Hello, #{name}!"
end

# Method with keyword arguments
def create_user(name:, email:, age: 0)
  { name: name, email: email, age: age }
end

create_user(name: "Bob", email: "bob@example.com")

# Return values (explicit or implicit)
def add(a, b)
  a + b  # implicit return
end

def find_even(numbers)
  numbers.find { |n| n.even? }  # returns found element or nil
end

# Splat operator for variable arguments
def sum(*numbers)
  numbers.sum
end

sum(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)  # => 15
box.title: Creating and using methods

box.code:
# Blocks (most common)
[1, 2, 3].each { |n| puts n }      # single line block
[1, 2, 3].each do |n|              # multi-line block
  puts n
  puts n * 2
end

# Yield and block_given?
def with_logging
  puts "Starting..."
  yield if block_given?
  puts "Completed!"
end

with_logging { puts "Processing..." }

# Procs (reusable blocks)
square = Proc.new { |x| x * x }
[1, 2, 3].map(&square)  # => [1, 4, 9]

# Lambdas (like procs with argument checking)
double = ->(x) { x * 2 }
[1, 2, 3].map(&double)  # => [2, 4, 6]

# Differences:
# - Lambdas check argument count
# - return in lambda returns from lambda
# - return in proc returns from enclosing method
box.title: Ruby's closure types

box.code:
# Arrays
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "orange"]
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Array operations
fruits[0]           # => "apple"
fruits << "grape"   # add element
fruits.push("kiwi") # add element
fruits.pop()        # remove last element
fruits.length       # => 4
fruits.include?("apple") # => true

# Hashes (key-value pairs)
person = {
  name: "Alice",
  age: 30,
  city: "New York"
}

# Hash operations
person[:name] = "Bob"
person[:email] = "bob@example.com"
person.keys     # => [:name, :age, :city, :email]
person.values   # => ["Bob", 30, "New York", "bob@example.com"]

# Symbols vs Strings
:string_object  # same object each time
"string_object" # different object each time
box.title: Core data structures

box.code:
# Common enumerable methods
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Transformation
numbers.map { |n| n * 2 }        # => [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]
numbers.select { |n| n.even? }   # => [2, 4]
numbers.reject { |n| n.even? }   # => [1, 3, 5]
numbers.find { |n| n > 3 }       # => 4

# Aggregation
numbers.reduce(:+)               # => 15
numbers.sum                      # => 15
numbers.max                      # => 5
numbers.min                      # => 1

# Querying
numbers.any? { |n| n > 3 }      # => true
numbers.all? { |n| n > 0 }      # => true
numbers.include?(3)             # => true

# Sorting and grouping
[3, 1, 4, 2].sort              # => [1, 2, 3, 4]
[1, 2, 3, 4].group_by(&:even?) # => {false=>[1, 3], true=>[2, 4]}
box.title: Powerful iteration methods

box.code:
# Class definition
class Person
  # Constructor
  def initialize(name, age)
    @name = name
    @age = age
  end

  # Instance methods
  def introduce
    "Hi, I'm #{@name} and I'm #{@age} years old."
  end

  # Attribute accessors
  attr_reader :name    # getter
  attr_writer :age     # setter
  attr_accessor :city  # both getter and setter

  # Class method
  def self.species
    "Homo sapiens"
  end
end

# Creating objects
alice = Person.new("Alice", 30)
puts alice.introduce  # => "Hi, I'm Alice and I'm 30 years old."
puts Person.species   # => "Homo sapiens"

# Everything is an object
5.class              # => Integer
"hello".class        # => String
[1, 2, 3].class      # => Array
nil.class            # => NilClass
box.title: Ruby is purely object-oriented

box.code:
# Inheritance
class Employee < Person
  def initialize(name, age, position)
    super(name, age)  # call parent constructor
    @position = position
  end

  def work
    "#{@name} is working as #{@position}"
  end
end

# Modules as mixins
module Loggable
  def log(message)
    puts "[LOG] #{Time.now}: #{message}"
  end
end

class Service
  include Loggable
  
  def perform
    log("Service started")
    # do work
    log("Service completed")
  end
end

# Duck typing (focus on behavior, not type)
def process_item(item)
  if item.respond_to?(:process)
    item.process
  else
    "Cannot process this item"
  end
end
box.title: Code organization and reuse

box.code:
# Basic exception handling
begin
  # Code that might raise an exception
  result = 10 / 0
rescue ZeroDivisionError => e
  puts "Cannot divide by zero: #{e.message}"
rescue StandardError => e
  puts "An error occurred: #{e.message}"
else
  # Runs if no exception was raised
  puts "Success: #{result}"
ensure
  # Always runs, for cleanup
  puts "Execution completed"
end

# Raising exceptions
def divide(a, b)
  raise ArgumentError, "Divisor cannot be zero" if b.zero?
  a / b
end

# Custom exception classes
class ValidationError < StandardError
  def initialize(field)
    super("Invalid value for field: #{field}")
  end
end

# Using custom exceptions
begin
  raise ValidationError.new("email")
rescue ValidationError => e
  puts "Validation failed: #{e.message}"
end
box.title: Managing errors in Ruby

box.code:
# Built-in debugging
puts "Value: #{variable}"        # Simple output
p variable                       # Inspect with .inspect
pp variable                      # Pretty print

# Using byebug (add to Gemfile)
require 'byebug'

def calculate_total
  price = 100
  tax = 20
  byebug  # execution stops here
  total = price + tax
  total
end

# Using pry (more powerful)
require 'pry'

def complex_method
  data = fetch_data
  binding.pry  # opens interactive console
  process(data)
end

# Common pry commands
# ls          - list methods
# cd variable - navigate into object
# whereami    - show current context
# exit        - continue execution

# Debugging tips:
# 1. Use binding.pry for complex issues
# 2. Use byebug for step-by-step debugging
# 3. Use puts/p for quick inspection
box.title: Ruby debugging ecosystem

box.code:
# Gemfile example
source 'https://rubygems.org'

ruby '3.2.2'

gem 'rails', '~> 7.0.0'
gem 'pg', '~> 1.1'
gem 'puma', '~> 5.0'
gem 'redis', '~> 4.0'

# Development and test gems
group :development, :test do
  gem 'debug', '~> 1.0'
  gem 'rspec-rails', '~> 6.0'
end

group :test do
  gem 'capybara', '~> 3.0'
end

# Bundler commands
bundle install        # Install dependencies
bundle update         # Update dependencies
bundle exec command   # Run command in bundle context
bundle info gem_name  # Show gem information
bundle init           # Create new Gemfile
box.title: Managing dependencies

box.code:
# IRB - Interactive Ruby Shell
$ irb
irb(main):001> 2 + 2
=> 4
irb(main):002> "hello".upcase
=> "HELLO"
irb(main):003> [1, 2, 3].map { |n| n * 2 }
=> [2, 4, 6]

# Enhanced IRB with pry
$ gem install pry
$ pry

# RuboCop - Code style enforcement
# .rubocop.yml configuration
Style/Documentation:
  Enabled: false

Metrics/LineLength:
  Max: 120

# Running RuboCop
rubocop                    # Check all files
rubocop app/models         # Check specific directory
rubocop --auto-correct     # Auto-fix issues
rubocop --format offenses  # Show summary
box.title: Interactive Ruby and Code Quality

box.code:
# Install Rails
gem install rails

# Create new application
rails new my_app --database=postgresql
cd my_app

# Generate scaffold (CRUD operations)
rails generate scaffold Product name:string price:decimal description:text

# Run migrations
rails db:create
rails db:migrate

# Start server
rails server
# Visit http://localhost:3000/products

# Key directories
app/           # Models, Views, Controllers
db/            # Database migrations and schema
config/        # Application configuration
test/          # Test files
public/        # Static files
box.title: Getting started with Rails

box.code:
# spec/models/user_spec.rb
require 'rails_helper'

RSpec.describe User, type: :model do
  describe 'validations' do
    it 'validates presence of email' do
      user = User.new(email: nil)
      expect(user).not_to be_valid
      expect(user.errors[:email]).to include("can't be blank")
    end

    it 'validates uniqueness of email' do
      create(:user, email: 'test@example.com')
      user = build(:user, email: 'test@example.com')
      expect(user).not_to be_valid
    end
  end

  describe '#full_name' do
    it 'returns first and last name' do
      user = build(:user, first_name: 'John', last_name: 'Doe')
      expect(user.full_name).to eq('John Doe')
    end
  end

  describe 'scopes' do
    it 'returns active users' do
      active_user = create(:user, active: true)
      inactive_user = create(:user, active: false)
      expect(User.active).to include(active_user)
      expect(User.active).not_to include(inactive_user)
    end
  end
end
box.title: Behavior-Driven Development

box.code:
# Variables & Methods
name = "Alice"
def greet(name) = "Hello, #{name}!"

# Arrays & Hashes
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
person = { name: "John", age: 30 }

# Control Flow
if condition; end
unless condition; end
case value; when 1; end

# Loops
5.times { |i| puts i }
[1,2,3].each { |n| puts n }
while condition; end

# Common Enumerables
map, select, reject, find, reduce
sort, group_by, any?, all?, include?

# Classes & Modules
class Person; end
module Loggable; end
attr_accessor :name

# Error Handling
begin; rescue; ensure; end
raise "Error message"
